content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

April 21, 1997

Steven S. Lucas, Esq.
Nielsen, Merksamer,
Parrinello, Mueller & Naylor
591 Redwood Highway, #4000
Mill Valley, California 94941

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-167

Dear Mr. Lucas:

This letter is a response to your request on behalf of Yes on Proposition 8 (committee
identification no. 9622872) and Citizens for a Better Los Angeles (committee identification no.
962873) for advice regarding the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS²

1. "If a ballot measure committee receives a $25,000 contribution from a contributor
during 1997, followed by a second $25,000 contribution from the same contributor, and the
ballot measure committee then refunds $1,000 of the latter contribution to the contributor, is that
contributor considered to have made "cumulative contributions of $50,000 or more" therefore
requiring subsequent (following the refund) identification on a disclosure statement in ballot
measure advertisements under section 84503, as recently enacted by Proposition 208 (provided
that the contributor is the highest or second highest)?"

Proposition 208 added a new Article 5, "Disclosure in Advertisements," to Chapter 4 of
the Act. Among other things, the new article requires that "[a]ny advertisement for or against a
ballot measure include a disclosure statement identifying any person whose cumulative

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

² Specifically, you ask questions 1 and 2 on behalf of Yes on Proposition 8. The full name of Yes on
Proposition 8 is: YES ON PROPOSITION 8, a coalition including Mayor Richard Riordan, Seniors, Taxpayers and
other Citizens concerned about good government who gratefully acknowledge the support of diverse Angelenos,
including Selim Zilkha, natural resources, and entertainment and media. Question 3 is asked on behalf of Citizens
for a Better Los Angeles.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-97-167
Page 2

contributions are fifty thousand dollars ($50,000) or more." (Section 84503(a); subdivision (b)
of Section 84503 provides for situations in which there are more than two such cumulative
contributors.)

The term "cumulative contributions" is defined in Section 84502:

"'Cumulative contributions' means the cumulative contributions to a
committee beginning the first day the statement of organization is filed under
Section 84101 and ending within seven days of the time the advertisement is sent
to the printer or broadcast station."

Section 84502 addresses only the time-frame in which contributions received from a particular
contributor are cumulated; it merely specifies when that time-frame begins and ends. It makes
no changes whatsoever to the substantive definition of "contribution," nor to the well-settled rule
about what when a given contribution is considered to be received by the committee.

For present purposes,³ that rule is: "A monetary contribution is 'received' on the date that
the candidate or committee, or the agent of the candidate or committee, obtains possession or
control of the check or other negotiable instrument by which the contribution is made."
(Regulation 18421.1(c).) There are two exceptions to this rule. First, a contribution is not
deemed accepted if it is not cashed, negotiated, or deposited and is returned to the contributor
before the closing date of the campaign statement on which it would otherwise be reported.
(Section 84211(q).) The second exception is in Section 84203(c), which provides that a late
contribution⁴ shall not be deemed accepted if it is not cashed, negotiated, or deposited and is
returned to the contributor within 24 hours. If neither of these exceptions apply, then a given
contribution is deemed received and accepted upon negotiation or deposit.

Your client has received, negotiated, and deposited two contributions from a particular
contributor. Under well-settled, long-standing law, your client accepted the two contributions.
There is no provision in the Act, the regulations, nor in the Commission's advice for "undoing"
part of a monetary contribution once it is deemed accepted. Your letter argues that such a
provision for "undoing" monetary contributions should be read into Section 84502. It is unlikely
that the Commission has the authority to read such an exception in the statute. However, even if
it does have such authority, it has yet to exercise it in the way you suggest.

First, contrary to the assertion in your letter that such an exception is "dictated by the
express language of Section 84503," neither that section nor Section 84502 alters in any way the
Act's substantive definition of contribution; nor does either section implicate the regulatory

³ There is an alternate rule for contributions received via payroll deductions or membership dues which is
irrelevant to this analysis. (See Regulation 18421.1(d).)

⁴ See Section 82036.

File No. A-97-167
Page 3

definition of when a contribution is deemed received and accepted. Thus, there is no basis in the
plain language of the statute for the policy you recommend.

Second, your primary argument is grounded in speculation about "dirty tricks" involving
unwanted contributions from unsavory parties orchestrated by a committee's political opponents.
You argue that absent the adoption of your recommendation, a committee which "unwittingly"
deposited such a contribution would be powerless to defend itself against such skullduggery.
Even if there is merit to such speculation, it is irrelevant to your client's situation. They do not
wish to return the entire contribution of an unwanted contributor from whom they wish to
distance themselves. They wish only to return that minimal portion of a contribution from a
contributor with whom they are otherwise quite content to be associated to the tune of
$49,000.

Finally, the equities of the situation do not appear to favor your client. They wish to
return only a small part of one contribution, two percent of the total amount received from the
contributor. In context, it appears that their intent is to return the minimum necessary to avoid
their disclosure obligations under the Act. We decline to give advice which facilitates this
apparent intent. Therefore, regardless of the refunded portion of the second $25,000
contribution, the contributor referred to in your letter is considered to have made cumulative
contributions of $50,000 or more, and the advertising disclosure provisions of Section 84503 are
triggered as to that contributor.

2. "Does the same rule apply to new section 84504, which requires that a ballot measure
committee 'name and identify itself using a name or phrase that clearly identifies the economic
or other special interest of its major donors of $50,000 or more?'"

Yes, please see the response to question no. 1.

3. "Does an independent expenditure committee consider its pre-1997 contributions
(received prior to the effective date of Proposition 208) in determining its two largest
contributors who must be disclosed in broadcast and mass mailing advertisements under section
84506, as recently enacted by Proposition 208? Does an independent expenditure committee
consider pre-1997 contributions when calculation $50,000 donors under sections 84503 and
84504?"

Other than the provisions of Proposition 208 addressing the retirement of campaign debt
incurred before January 1, 1997 (see Section 85305(c)), the Commission has consistently
interpreted Proposition 208 not to have retroactive application. (See, e.g., the Brusavich Advice
Letter, No. I-96-350.) Therefore, contributions received before January 1, 1997, are not
considered for purposes of Sections 84503, 84504, and 84506.

File No. A-97-167
Page 4

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: John Vergelli
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:JV:ak
